title: Follow-up on the AI trading agent I tested earlier
author: emlanis
selftext:
I posted here about a DeFi AI trading bot I was testing when it was still on testnet.

Small follow-up after checking the newer version.

The biggest change is that it now feels less like a demo and more like an actual trading assistant. The flow is still Telegram-based, but the command set is more complete now.

You can:

\- check wallet balances and funding info

\- set trading rules for the bot to follow

\- switch between auto and assist mode

\- see portfolio status and last decision

\- check logs and audit entries

\- close a position manually

\- ask it things in plain language instead of only using commands

I still don’t see this as **AI prints money.**

The interesting part is the interface shift.

Most trading bots either give signals or automate basic execution. This one feels more like telling an agent what kind of position you want, what rules it should respect, and when it should stay hands-off.

That could be useful, but it also creates new questions:

\- how well does it follow user instructions during volatility?

\- how clear are the reasons behind each decision?

\- how much control should a user give to an agent?

\- what happens when market conditions move faster than the bot’s cycle?

For now, I’m more interested in the design than the performance.

Onchain trading with agents is probably coming either way. The part that matters is whether these tools make risk easier to manage, or just make people lose money faster with a nicer interface.

Curious if anyone here is testing similar AI trading agents that actually execute, not just send signals.
